content.text:
Agent orientation
First-turn context priming for new agent sessions. You are analyzing an unfamiliar github repository.
Do a thorough but focused investigation to infer the following:

* Repo structure & purpose
* Load-bearing sections
* Git history signals
* Anti-overfitting guidance: overfit is grounding that raises the agent's confidence faster than it raises its verification — the agent acts on what x asserts without touching the file x pointed at.

Return a concise structured report for the user with:

* Executive summary (2-3 sentences)
* Git history insights with specific commit refs or date ranges where helpful
* Smells (Optional, report if present only): brief statements on "this caught my eye as out of place and the human should be aware"
Analyzing the repo. Let me pull structure and git history in parallel.

1 # Omni-Scale 2 3 Self-hosted Sidero Omni deployment kit with Tailscale authentication and Proxmox infrastructure provider. 4 5 ## Repo Boundary 6 7 This repo answers: how is the Omni-managed Talos substrate created and operated? 8 9 Omni-Scale owns the self-hosted Omni Hub, the Proxmox infrastructure provider, 10 cluster templates, machine classes, Talos node requirements, SideroLink/DNS/Tailscale 11 access constraints, and the handoff into GitOps. 12 13 It does not own post-bootstrap platform applications. Longhorn Helm values, 14 backup schedules, StorageClasses, ArgoCD app waves, ESO app wiring, Tailscale 15 Operator manifests, monitoring, dashboards, and workload exposure live in 16 `../mothership-gitops`. This repo may document the substrate requirement that 17 enables those systems, such as Talos worker mount patches for Longhorn, but 18 the GitOps repo is the source of truth for their implementation and operation. 19 20 ## What This Does 21 22 Omni-Scale deploys Sidero Omni for managing Talos Linux Kubernetes clusters on Proxmox 23 infrastructure. The architecture separates the control plane (Omni Hub) from the VM 24 provisioner (Proxmox Provider), connected via Tailscale. 25 26 27 28 29 The Provider sits on the same L2 network as Talos VMs — this is required for SideroLink registration during boot. 30 31 ## Repository Structure 32 33 This is a reference implementation. Each directory has its own README with details. 34 35 | Directory | Purpose | 36 |-----------|---------| 37 | `omni/` | Omni Hub Docker Compose stack | 38 | `proxmox-provider/` | Proxmox infrastructure provider | 39 | `clusters/` | Cluster templates (Omni format) | 40 | `machine-classes/` | VM sizing definitions | 41 | `docs/` | Deployment, operations, troubleshooting | 42 43 ## Documentation 44 45 | Document | Purpose | 46 |----------|---------| 47 | [DEPLOYMENT.md](docs/DEPLOYMENT.md) | Full installation runbook | 48 | [OPERATIONS.md](docs/OPERATIONS.md) | Day-to-day management | 49 | [TROUBLESHOOTING.md](docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md) | Issue resolution | 50 | [CILIUM.md](docs/guides/CILIUM.md) | CNI installation post-bootstrap | 51 52 ## Critical Gotchas 53 54 These cost days of debugging. Don't repeat them. 55 56 ### Cilium MTU Auto-Detect (The Silent Assassin) 57 58 Unlike the other gotchas in this list, this one ran for months without anyone 59 noticing. Tailscale Ingress throughput sat at ~22 KB/s instead of ~99 Mbps 60 (commit `e0d5f5e`). No errors. No alerts. Just slow web pages. 61 62 Omni's `siderolink` WireGuard tunnel runs at MTU 1280 on every Talos node 63 (`talosctl get links siderolink -o yaml` → `mtu: 1280, kind: wireguard`). 64 Without `--set MTU=1450` at Cilium install, pod networking ends up at 1280. 65 The exact Cilium auto-detect path that resolves to 1280 on Talos is not 66 mapped — see CILIUM.md for what is and isn't documented. 67 68 **Fix**: Install and upgrade Cilium with `--set MTU=1450` (1500 NIC minus 50 69 VXLAN overhead). For a live cluster already affected, patch `cilium-config` 70 and restart the Cilium DaemonSets plus the `tailscale-operator` namespace 71 StatefulSets — see [CILIUM.md](docs/guides/CILIUM.md) and 72 [TROUBLESHOOTING.md](docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md). The ConfigMap patch does not 73 survive a future `cilium install`; update the runbooks too. 74 75 ### Split-Horizon DNS (The Four-Day Boss Fight) 76 77 Talos VMs must reach Omni via LAN IP during boot — they're not on Tailscale yet. If Proxmox 78 hosts use public DNS (1.1.1.1), VMs inherit it and resolve to the Tailscale IP they can't 79 reach. 80

content.text:
# Omni-Scale — Orientation Report

## Executive summary

Omni-Scale is the deployment kit for a self-hosted Sidero Omni instance (Talos Linux cluster lifecycle manager) with Tailscale-fronted auth, targeting the "Matrix" production Proxmox cluster. There is almost no application code here — the load-bearing artifacts are two Docker Compose stacks ([omni/compose.yml](omni/compose.yml), [proxmox-provider/compose.yml](proxmox-provider/compose.yml)), the Omni cluster template [talos-prod-01.yaml](clusters/talos-prod-01.yaml) (3 CP + 3 workers, CNI disabled for post-bootstrap Cilium), and the `machine-classes/` VM sizing definitions. Everything else is documentation encoding hard-won operational knowledge, and that documentation is genuinely the product: the README's "Critical Gotchas" (Cilium MTU 1280 silent throughput collapse, split-horizon DNS, the `docker compose down -v` footgun) are the highest-value content in the repo.

The repo boundary is explicit and disciplined: this repo owns substrate creation (bare Omni → registered Talos VMs → bootstrap), and `../mothership-gitops` owns everything after the GitOps handoff. Several commits (`7d547c3`, `f1c9080`, `aee8e77`) exist purely to enforce that split — it's a deliberate architectural decision, not an accident.

## Git history insights

Activity is bimodal. The build phase was **Dec 2025–Jan 2026** (63 of 91 commits): initial commit `f9912c9` on 2025-12-27, then rapid iteration through January including two structural refactors — `f8061b5` (repo layout, killed `PLAN.md`) and `1ee82b5` (2026-01-11, migrated `specs/` and Claude artifacts out to an "omni-scale plugin"). **Feb–Jun 2026** is near-dormant maintenance dominated by renovate[bot] (16 commits, mostly mise tool bumps). **July 2026** shows renewed activity: `53db1ca` (2026-07-09, "repair Omni recovery tooling") substantially rewrote [disaster-recovery.sh](scripts/disaster-recovery.sh) (456 lines changed) and the `provider-ctl.py` helper, followed by CI hardening (`04bc36f`) and Entire-hooks adoption (`11aa72e`, HEAD).

Churn leaders are `mise.toml` (17), `.gitignore` (11), and `docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md` (9) — the troubleshooting churn tells you this repo learns by getting burned in production. `e0d5f5e` (Cilium MTU=1450 override) and `cc0afee` (Talos v1.12.1 bump) are the notable substrate-state commits. `docs/audits/AUDIT_REPORT_2026-04-28.md` shows a docs-vs-reality audit was already run once in April.

## Anti-overfitting notes

Two places where documentation asserts things you should verify before acting. First, the docs stack is large relative to the artifacts and has drifted before (that's why the April audit exists) — when a runbook names a command, check `mise.toml` and the actual scripts, since `53db1ca` rewrote recovery tooling well after most docs were written. Second, `CLAUDE.md` is a symlink to `AGENTS.md` — edits to either hit both agent ecosystems, so don't treat them as independent files. Third, the history references files that no longer exist (`specs/*.yaml`, `PLAN.md`); if any doc or memory points at `specs/`, it left in `1ee82b5` for the plugin repo.

## Smells

- **Dirty working tree**: `mise.toml` has an uncommitted deletion of `eza = "0.23.5"` from `[tools]`. Either commit it or drop it — an unexplained diff sitting on `main` in an infra repo is exactly how "which version was actually deployed" questions start.
- **Unmerged renovate branches**: `origin/renovate/talos-cluster` and `origin/renovate/github-actions` are open remotely. The talos-cluster one matters — a pending Talos/K8s version bump against a production cluster template shouldn't sit unreviewed.
- **`tailscale:latest` pinned only by digest** in [omni/compose.yml](omni/compose.yml) — functionally pinned (renovate manages the sha), fine, but the `:latest` tag is cosmetically misleading if you read the file without noticing the digest.
- **Heavy AI-tooling surface** for a compose-and-YAML repo: `.agents/`, `.claude/`, `.codex/`, `.cursor/`, `.pi/`, `.entire/`, `skills-lock.json`. Consistent with the 3I workspace design, so not wrong — but the `omni-talos` skill under `.agents/skills/` duplicates operational knowledge that also lives in `docs/`, which is two homes for one workflow and a drift risk the April audit already had to clean up once.
Unmerged renovate branches:  - excatly why i'm here, lets dig into it

content.text:
Full picture assembled. Here's what's actually going on with those branches.

## The verdict: PR #3 is unmergeable, and has been for four months

The renovate PR ([#3](https://github.com/basher83/Omni-Scale/pull/3), open since 2026-03-08) wants [talos-prod-01.yaml](clusters/talos-prod-01.yaml) bumped from Talos v1.12.1 → v1.13.6 and Kubernetes v1.35.0 → v1.36.2. I queried your live Omni at `omni.spaceships.work` (it re-authed my expired CLI key against your account): the backend is **v1.4.6**, its `talosversions` registry contains **zero 1.13.x entries** — it tops out at 1.12.1 — and 1.12.1's Kubernetes compatibility list ends at 1.35.6. Both halves of the PR would be rejected by the running control plane. Merging it accomplishes nothing except making the repo lie about the substrate.

The root cause is structural: renovate watches the cluster template but is blind to the Omni server version. `OMNI_IMG_TAG=v1.4.6` lives in the gitignored `omni.env` (mirrored in [omni.env.example](omni/omni.env.example)), and [compose.yml](omni/compose.yml) only references `${OMNI_IMG_TAG}`, so no manager ever proposes an Omni upgrade. Meanwhile Omni upstream marched to **v1.9.3** (2026-07-16) — you're five minors behind, and the gate on every Talos/K8s bump is the piece nothing tracks. Even Talos *patch* updates are blocked: 1.12.10 exists upstream but your Omni doesn't know anything past 1.12.1.

## Upgrade path assessment

The good news: **1.4.6 → 1.9.3 direct is supported.** v1.6.0's urgent upgrade notes only require stepping stones if you're upgrading from *older than* v1.4.0 (the SQLite migration era) — you're past that, and your compose already uses the modern `--sqlite-storage-path` flag. Talos 1.13.0 machinery landed in Omni ~v1.6.5–1.7.0 (Talos 1.13.0 released 2026-04-27), so v1.9.3 comfortably covers 1.13.6. The one breaking change that could touch this repo is v1.8.0's `omnictl cluster template` restriction on including files outside the current directory — but I checked, and your active templates use only inline patches; `file:` references exist solely in commented examples and README docs. No impact.

Also relevant: the freshly-pushed `renovate/mise-tools` branch bumps `omnictl` 1.9.1 → 1.9.3, which would exactly match the post-upgrade server. The current 1.9.1-client-vs-1.4.6-server skew already warns on every invocation.

## Recommended sequence

1. **Merge [#14](https://github.com/basher83/Omni-Scale/pull/14)** (github-actions digest pins) — trivial, zero risk, do it now.
2. **Resolve the dirty `mise.toml`** (uncommitted `eza` deletion) so the mise-tools PR rebases cleanly, then take that bump too.
3. **Upgrade the Omni Hub on Holly**: read the "Urgent Upgrade Notes" sections for v1.5.0/1.6.0/1.7.0/1.8.0/1.9.0 (I've summarized the load-bearing ones above), set `OMNI_IMG_TAG=v1.9.3` in `omni.env`, `docker compose pull && docker compose up -d` — and per your own gotcha list, never `down -v`. Update the example file and commit so the repo reflects reality. Verify with `omnictl get talosversions | grep 1.13`.
4. **Then take PR #3 — but split the rollout.** Omni's template sync does orchestrate Talos-before-K8s, but on a 6-node production cluster I'd sync Talos 1.13.6 first, verify (especially Cilium/Longhorn health — your iscsi/nfsd/util-linux extensions get rebuilt per-version via the factory), then take K8s 1.36.2. Note renovate's branch only touched `talos-prod-01.yaml`, not [test-cluster.yaml](clusters/test/test-cluster.yaml) — if you want a rehearsal on the test cluster, bump it by hand first.
5. **Fix the blind spot** so this doesn't recur: add a renovate custom regex manager for `OMNI_IMG_TAG` in `omni.env.example` (datasource `github-releases`, depName `siderolabs/omni`). Then the actual gating dependency shows up as a PR instead of rotting invisibly while its downstream PRs pile up.

One smell from the live check worth stating plainly: my omnictl auth key had expired and transparently re-registered a new PGP key for your account mid-command. Convenient, but it means anything with your Tailscale reach can mint Omni credentials — that's your ACL doing the access control, which is presumably the design, but worth being conscious of.

that's your ACL doing the access control, which is presumably the design, but worth being conscious of - no i actually had to manually approve via web ui - thats the design. 

Lets execute 1/2 those are easy ones.

Noted — key registration is gated by manual web-UI approval, not just tailnet reach. That's the right design; correction accepted.

Both done, everything green.

**Step 1** — [#14](https://github.com/basher83/Omni-Scale/pull/14) squash-merged as `a8d7990` (matching the repo's renovate merge convention), branch deleted. CI passed on the merge commit.

**Step 2** — `mise.toml` cleaned up and bumped in `f4ed067`, pushed to main with CI green:

- Committed your pending `eza` removal (it wasn't referenced by any task, so nothing broke).
- Applied the `renovate/mise-tools` versions directly: rg 15.2.0, rumdl 0.2.37, uv 0.11.29, and `omnictl` 1.9.3 — the last one deliberately matching the Omni v1.9.3 upgrade target, so the client/server skew disappears the moment you upgrade the Hub. Renovate will see its branch is obsolete and clean it up on its next run.
- Before committing I ran `mise install`, `mise run validate:omni`, and the full `mise run check` gate locally — all passed. Notably, omnictl **1.9.3** validates both cluster templates fine, which confirms the v1.8 `cluster template` file-inclusion breaking change doesn't affect your inline-patch templates.

Remaining from the sequence: step 3 (Omni Hub v1.4.6 → v1.9.3 on Holly — the actual unlock), step 4 (PR #3, split Talos-then-K8s), and step 5 (renovate custom manager for `OMNI_IMG_TAG` so the Hub version stops rotting invisibly). Step 3 touches the running management plane, so that one's yours to schedule — say the word when you want the upgrade runbook laid out.
for the record the cmds are: docker compose --env-file omni.env pull && docker compose --env-file omni.env up -d --force-recreate - omni hub is updated

## 7. Day 2 Operations

### Upgrade Omni Hub

The Hub version gates which Talos and Kubernetes versions Omni will accept —
upgrade it before bumping cluster templates. Read the "Urgent Upgrade Notes"
in the [Omni release notes](https://github.com/siderolabs/omni/releases) for
every minor version you skip.

On the Omni host, from `omni/`:

```bash
# Set the new version
$EDITOR omni.env   # OMNI_IMG_TAG=vX.Y.Z

# --env-file is required: compose interpolates ${OMNI_IMG_TAG} at parse
# time and the env file is not named .env. Never use `down -v` (deletes
# Tailscale state).
docker compose --env-file omni.env pull
docker compose --env-file omni.env up -d --force-recreate
```

Verify:

```bash
omnictl get sysversion -o jsonpath='{.spec.backendversion}'
omnictl get talosversions   # new versions should appear
```

Keep `omni.env.example` and the `omnictl` pin in `mise.toml` in sync with
the deployed version.

### Upgrade Talos Nodes

content.text:
Hub upgrade verified and now on the record. State of play:

- Live backend reports **v1.9.3**; `talosversions` now lists 1.13.0–1.13.6, and 1.13.6's compatibility list includes Kubernetes 1.36.0–1.36.2. **PR [#3](https://github.com/basher83/Omni-Scale/pull/3) is fully unblocked** — every version it proposes is now accepted by the control plane.
- Pushed `94a744a`: your exact commands are documented in a new "Upgrade Omni Hub" section in [OPERATIONS.md](docs/OPERATIONS.md) under Day 2 Operations — including *why* `--env-file` is mandatory (compose resolves `${OMNI_IMG_TAG}` at parse time and the file isn't named `.env`, so without the flag you'd pull an empty tag) and the standing `down -v` prohibition. Also synced [omni.env.example](omni/omni.env.example) to v1.9.3. Quality gate passed before push.

That closes steps 1–3. What's left:

**Step 4** — the production rollout itself. My recommendation stands: don't merge #3 and sync both bumps in one shot. Edit the template to Talos 1.13.6 first, sync, let all six nodes roll and verify Cilium/Longhorn health, then take K8s 1.36.2. Renovate will mark #3 merged-by-hand either way. This rolls Matrix, so it fires when you say so.

**Step 5** — the renovate custom manager for `OMNI_IMG_TAG` in `omni.env.example`, so the Hub version drift that caused this whole four-month stall shows up as a PR next time. That one's pure repo config and I can knock it out now if you want it.

Step 5 - having it pop as a pr is a good call, just realize that really only flags it to tell us that i have to update it. theres no auto update action or anyhting ... which is what we should actually do tbh

Right — a PR that nobody's host reads is just a nag with extra steps. The actual fix has two parts: make the version flag natively (no custom regex manager needed), and make merging the PR *be* the deploy. But blind auto-update on the management plane is where I draw the line: Omni ships "Urgent Upgrade Notes" per minor for a reason, and Quantum's whole design principle is that the management plane doesn't get to surprise you. Merge-gated deploy is the right shape — human reads release notes in the PR, merge applies it.

First, the repo-side piece that's unambiguous. Renovate already proved it can natively pin images in this exact compose file (it did `tailscale/tailscale` in `b86d564`) — the only reason it can't see Omni is that the tag hides behind `${OMNI_IMG_TAG}`. Pin it literally and the custom manager becomes unnecessary:

# Omni Environment Configuration
# Copy to omni.env and fill in actual values

# =============================================================================
# Host Configuration
# =============================================================================
# LAN IP where Omni will bind ports (must be reachable by Talos VMs)
# Holly: 192.168.10.20
HOST_IP=192.168.10.20

# =============================================================================
# Omni Version & Identity
# =============================================================================
OMNI_IMG_TAG=v1.4.6
OMNI_ACCOUNT_UUID=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
NAME=omni
EVENT_SINK_PORT=8091

# =============================================================================
# Keys and Certificates
# =============================================================================
# TLS certificates (Let's Encrypt or self-signed)
TLS_CERT=/etc/letsencrypt/live/omni.spaceships.work/fullchain.pem
TLS_KEY=/etc/letsencrypt/live/omni.spaceships.work/privkey.pem

# etcd storage and encryption
ETCD_VOLUME_PATH=/etc/etcd/
ETCD_ENCRYPTION_KEY=/path/to/omni.asc

# SQLite database path (CRITICAL for v1.4+)
OMNI_SQLITE_PATH=/_out/etcd/omni.db

# =============================================================================
# Network Binding
# =============================================================================
BIND_ADDR=0.0.0.0:443
MACHINE_API_BIND_ADDR=0.0.0.0:8090
K8S_PROXY_BIND_ADDR=0.0.0.0:8100

# =============================================================================
# Advertised URLs
# =============================================================================
# External access (via Tailscale) - Browser/UI
ADVERTISED_API_URL="https://omni.spaceships.work"
ADVERTISED_K8S_PROXY_URL="https://omni.spaceships.work:8100/"

# Internal access (LAN) - Talos Nodes via SideroLink
# Use the LAN IP where Omni is reachable by Talos VMs
SIDEROLINK_ADVERTISED_API_URL="https://omni.spaceships.work:8090/"
SIDEROLINK_WIREGUARD_ADVERTISED_ADDR="192.168.10.20:50180"

# =============================================================================
# Authentication
# =============================================================================
# Initial admin user(s) - comma-separated email addresses
INITIAL_USER_EMAILS="admin@example.com"

# Auth0 OIDC configuration
# Create application at: https://manage.auth0.com/
# Application type: Single Page Application
# Allowed callbacks: https://omni.spaceships.work/
AUTH='--auth-auth0-enabled=true --auth-auth0-domain=YOUR_TENANT.us.auth0.com --auth-auth0-client-id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID'

# =============================================================================
# Tailscale
# =============================================================================
# Generate auth key at: https://login.tailscale.com/admin/settings/keys
# Use reusable, ephemeral key with appropriate tags
TS_AUTHKEY="tskey-auth-XXXXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX"

Repo half is done and pushed (`0079d7d`). Renovate will now open Omni release PRs natively — same mechanism as the tailscale digest pin, no custom manager needed. `OMNI_IMG_TAG` is gone from compose, env example, and both docs.

Now the part you actually asked for — making the merge *do* something. Here's my position, then one question before I build.

**Blind auto-update is off the table.** Watchtower-style "always run latest" on the management plane is how you wake up to a broken Omni after an upgrade that had "Urgent Upgrade Notes (No, really, you MUST read this)" in bold. The gate should be the merge: renovate PR carries the release notes, you read them, you merge, the deploy happens. Human on the trigger, machine on the execution.

**Pull beats push.** A GitHub Action that joins the tailnet and SSHes into omni-host works, but it hands GitHub-hosted runners a credentialed path into your management plane — expanding the trust surface of the one host whose compromise owns every cluster. The pull model is a small reconciler *on omni-host*: a systemd timer that fetches the repo (it's public — no token, no inbound access, nothing new in GitHub secrets), diffs `omni/compose.yml` against the deployed copy, and on change runs the exact commands you dictated — `--env-file omni.env pull && up -d --force-recreate` — logging loudly. It's the same trust shape as ArgoCD: the plane reaches out, nothing reaches in.

Two things you should know before I wire it:

1. **First reconcile converges six months of drift.** The deployed `compose.yaml` (dated Jan 20) differs from the repo: floating `tailscale:stable` instead of the digest pin, no `env_file:` entries, hardcoded IPs. First run replaces it wholesale, which recreates the tailscale container — a brief Omni blip. That drift is the disease we're curing, but it means run one is not a no-op.
2. **The `ansible` user's fingerprints are on that directory** (`/home/ansible/docker/omni`, and DEPLOYMENT.md references `/home/ansible/...` paths). If an ansible playbook somewhere — Virgo-Core or elsewhere — considers itself the owner of that deploy dir, the reconciler belongs in *that* playbook (possibly as `ansible-pull` on a timer), not as a hand-dropped systemd unit that fights it. One workflow, one home.

So: does anything in your ansible estate currently own that directory, or was it a one-time hand deploy under the ansible user? If it's orphaned, I'll build the systemd reconciler on omni-host directly. If ansible owns it, point me at the playbook and I'll put the automation there.

It replaces and deprecates the previous method of setting environment variables via the `.machine.env` field.

Multiple values for the same environment variable will replace previous values, with the last one taking precedence.

To remove an environment variable, remove it from the `EnvironmentConfig` document and restart the node.
--
BREAKING: If you were relying on the resources EtcdConfigs, KubeletConfigs, ControllerManagerConfigs, SchedulerConfigs or APIServerConfigs, the protobuf format has changed from `map<string,string>` to `map<string,message>`.


### Container Image Signature Verification

--
### Install and Upgrade API

Talos now exposes install and upgrade operations via the `LifecycleService` API, enabling programmatic installs and upgrades through a single, consistent interface.
The legacy upgrade API is deprecated; new integrations should migrate to `LifecycleService` for future compatibility.


### Kubernetes server-side apply

--
It replaces and deprecates the previous method of configuring KubeSpan via the `.machine.network.kubespan` field.

The old configuration field will continue to work for backward compatibility.


--
See the documentation on [upgrade notes](https://docs.siderolabs.com/talos/v1.13/configure-your-talos-cluster/lifecycle-management/upgrading-talos#after-upgrade-to)
for more details on how to configure NVIDIA GPU support in Talos.


### Container Image Decompression
--
### Lifecycle Upgrade in talosctl

`talosctl` upgrades now route through `LifecycleService`, aligning CLI behavior with the new install/upgrade API and unifying the upgrade path.
This change is transparent to users but standardizes the backend used for upgrades.


### Component Updates

# Omni Substrate Update Rollout

Roll version updates through the substrate in dependency order: Omni Hub gates
Talos, Talos gates Kubernetes. The repo is desired state; the live backend is
truth. Never merge a version bump the running control plane would reject.

## Guardrails

- Never trust repo pins or PR titles for compatibility — verify against the
  live backend (`omnictl get talosversions`), which is the only authority on
  what Omni will accept.
- Desired state first: template edits are committed and pushed to main
  *before* `omnictl cluster template sync`. The cluster never runs anything
  main doesn't describe.
- Split rollouts: Talos and Kubernetes bumps are separate commits and separate
  syncs, with a health gate between. Never apply both halves in one sync.
- Never `docker compose down -v` on the Hub (deletes Tailscale state).
  Compose commands need `--env-file omni.env` (parse-time interpolation).
- Never migrate Talos VMs. A stuck node is destroy/recreate, per doctrine.
- The provider image `omni-infra-provider-proxmox:local-fix` is a locally
  patched fork — exempt from all version automation.

## Workflow

### 1. Triage

```bash
git fetch --prune
gh pr list --state open
git diff main...origin/renovate/<branch>   # for each renovate branch
```

Note PR age — a long-stale renovate PR usually means a hidden gate, not a
forgotten merge. Trivial PRs (action digest pins, mise tool bumps) merge
squash-style now; keep the `omnictl` mise pin matched to the Hub version.

### 2. Compatibility gate (live backend)

```bash
omnictl get sysversion -o jsonpath='{.spec.backendversion}'
omnictl get talosversions -o jsonpath='{.metadata.id}' | grep '^<target-talos>'
omnictl get talosversion <target-talos> -o yaml | grep '<target-k8s>'
```

If the target Talos/K8s versions are absent, the Hub is the blocker — do
step 3 first. If present, skip to step 4.

### 3. Hub upgrade (only when gated)

Read the "Urgent Upgrade Notes" in the Omni release notes for **every minor
being skipped**. Then follow the "Upgrade Omni Hub" runbook in
`docs/OPERATIONS.md` (version is pinned in `omni/compose.yml`, renovate PRs
it; deploy dir on omni-host is not a git clone — sync the file). Verify with
the step-2 commands, then re-run the gate.

### 4. Pre-flight (before touching the template)

- Talos release notes for the target minor: scan for breaking changes
  touching machine config, CNI, or extensions.
- Extensions exist for the target:
  `omnictl get talosextensions <ver> -o yaml | grep <each extension in template>`
- `omnictl get etcdbackupstatus` — if empty, flag it to the operator before a
  control-plane change; proceed only with acknowledgment.
- `omnictl cluster status <cluster>` — start from `RUNNING Ready`, all
  machines healthy. Never roll onto a degraded cluster.

### 5. Talos half

```bash
# edit clusters/<cluster>.yaml: talos.version only
omnictl cluster template validate -f clusters/<cluster>.yaml
git commit && git push          # desired state lands first
omnictl cluster template sync -v -f clusters/<cluster>.yaml
```

Monitor by polling `omnictl cluster status <cluster>`. For completion, check
per-machine versions — not the cluster Ready line, which races the next
phase:

```bash
omnictl get machinestatus -o json | jq -r '"\(.spec.network.hostname) \(.spec.talosversion)"'
```

### 6. Health gate

All must pass before the Kubernetes half:

```bash
kubectl get nodes                                        # all Ready, target Talos
kubectl -n kube-system get cm cilium-config -o jsonpath='{.data.mtu}'   # must be 1450
kubectl -n kube-system get ds cilium                     # N/N ready
kubectl -n longhorn-system get volumes.longhorn.io -o json | \
  jq -r '[.items[].status.robustness] | group_by(.) | map("\(.[0]): \(length)") | join(", ")'
```

Degraded Longhorn volumes after rolling reboots are normal — replicas
rebuilding. **Wait for 0 degraded** before proceeding; check engine
`rebuildStatus` for progress and errors. MTU not 1450 means the Cilium
config was clobbered — stop and fix per `docs/guides/CILIUM.md`.

### 7. Kubernetes half

Same as step 5 with `kubernetes.version`, its own commit, its own sync.
No node reboots — staged control-plane components then kubelets. Completion:
`kubectl get nodes` shows the target version on every node. Re-run the
step-6 health checks.

### 8. Close out

- Close the renovate PR with a comment stating what was applied and any
  ordering it required (`gh pr close <n> --comment "..." --delete-branch`),
  or let renovate auto-close on rebase.
- If the rollout changed operational commands or version pins anywhere else
  (docs, env examples, mise), sync them in the same session — stale copies
  of version facts are how the next stall starts.

## Failure handling

- Sync rejected for an unsupported version: the gate was skipped or raced —
  re-run step 2, do not retry the sync.
- A machine stuck mid-upgrade: investigate via Omni; if the node is wedged,
  destroy/recreate from spec. Never migrate, never hand-patch a node.
- Health gate fails: stop the rollout where it is. A half-rolled cluster in
  a known state beats a fully-rolled cluster in an unknown one.

# Omni Hub Upgrade

The Hub version gates which Talos and Kubernetes versions Omni accepts.
This skill upgrades the Hub itself. The human approves the plan; the agent
executes and verifies.

## Facts

- Desired state: the image tag pinned in `omni/compose.yml` (renovate opens a
  PR per Omni release, with release notes attached).
- Deployed state: `omni-host` (a VM — not Holly, which is the Proxmox host
  underneath), directory `/home/ansible/docker/omni`, deployed filename
  `compose.yaml`. It is **not** a git clone — the file must be synced to it.
- Access: `ssh root@omni-host`. `omnictl` runs from the workstation (mise pin).

## Preconditions

1. Read the "Urgent Upgrade Notes" for **every minor version being skipped**:

   ```bash
   gh api repos/siderolabs/omni/releases/tags/vX.Y.0 --jq .body | head -60
   ```

   Specifically check for removed CLI flags against the `command:` block in
   `omni/compose.yml` — a removed flag means a crash loop on boot.
2. Upgrading from older than v1.4.0 requires stepping through v1.4.x first
   (storage migrations). From v1.4.x onward, direct upgrades are supported.
3. State migrations are not guaranteed reversible. Treat rollback as
   unavailable; the plan is roll-forward. Present this in the plan for
   approval on multi-minor jumps.

## Procedure

1. Land desired state first: merge the renovate PR (or edit the pin in
   `omni/compose.yml`), commit, push.
2. Sync the compose file to the host:

   ```bash
   scp omni/compose.yml root@omni-host:/home/ansible/docker/omni/compose.yaml
   ```

3. Apply on the host. `--env-file` is mandatory (compose interpolates
   `${TS_AUTHKEY}` and volume paths at parse time; the env file is not named
   `.env`). **Never use `down -v`** — it deletes Tailscale state and causes
   hostname collisions.

   ```bash
   ssh root@omni-host 'cd /home/ansible/docker/omni && \
     docker compose --env-file omni.env pull && \
     docker compose --env-file omni.env up -d --force-recreate'
   ```

## Verify

All four, in order:

```bash
omnictl get sysversion -o jsonpath='{.spec.backendversion}'   # target version
omnictl get talosversions -o jsonpath='{.metadata.id}' | tail  # new versions appeared
omnictl get infraproviderstatus                                # provider re-registered
omnictl get machines                                           # all CONNECTED true
```

## Post-upgrade sync

Version facts live in multiple places; stale copies cause the next stall.
Update in the same session: the `omnictl` pin in `mise.toml` (client should
match backend), `omni/omni.env.example` if anything changed, and any doc
that names the old version.

## Failure handling

- Container crash-looping: `ssh root@omni-host 'docker logs omni --tail 50'`.
  An unknown-flag error means a removed flag was missed in preconditions —
  fix the `command:` block in the repo, re-sync, re-apply.
- UI/API unreachable but container healthy: check the tailscale sidecar
  (`docker logs omni-tailscale`); the omni container shares its network
  namespace.
- Provider not re-registering after upgrade: check the provider LXC
  (`omni-provider`), see the omni-talos skill for provider lifecycle.

content.text:
Done and pushed (`759768a`). Both skills live in `.agents/skills/`, quality gate green:

- **`omni-update-rollout`** — the approved draft, with step 3 now invoking the `omni-upgrade` skill instead of pointing at a doc.
- **`omni-upgrade`** — the Hub upgrade lifecycle, migrated out of OPERATIONS.md: release-note gating per skipped minor, the roll-forward-only warning for multi-minor jumps, exact deploy commands with the `--env-file` rationale, four-step verification, and the crash-loop/unreachable failure paths. OPERATIONS.md keeps a two-paragraph pointer; the skill owns the procedure.
- AGENTS.md now states the doctrine explicitly — docs describe the system, skills own the procedures — and the stale `omni-proxmox` entry is fixed.

For "and so on" — the rest of the lifecycle family, in the order I'd build them. **`omni-recovery`** first: `scripts/disaster-recovery.sh` and the recovery-procedures reference already exist, and DR is exactly where you want an agent following a tested procedure rather than improvising at 2am — the skill wraps destroy/recreate plus the GitOps re-bootstrap handoff. Then **`omni-backup`**: etcd backups are currently *unconfigured*, so this one starts as an implementation task (configure Omni's backup storage) and ends as an operating skill (verify/restore) — arguably it should exist before recovery ever gets exercised. Then **`omni-deploy`**: greenfield Hub bootstrap from DEPLOYMENT.md's 600-line runbook — biggest migration, least frequently needed. I'd hold off on a provider-convergence skill until the Virgo-Core reconciler work lands, since that session is actively changing what the procedure will be.

One structural decision that's yours: `omni-talos` is vendored from the `lunar-claude` plugin while the two new skills are authored here unmanaged. Eventually one home should win — either these migrate into the plugin and sync back via `skills-lock.json`, or the plugin's skill moves in-repo. Say which lifecycle skills to build and I'll draft them the same way.
